mappings.rationaleAI: Mexico's post-revolutionary regimes of Obregon and Calles (A.1), Cardenas reforms (A.2), WWII cooperation (A.3), and post-1945 PRI dominance, economic crises, and NAFTA (A.4) are comprehensively covered.

US relations with Mexico including immigration tensions and NAFTA (A.4.b), US involvement in Nicaragua including Contra insurgency (B.1.d), US relations with Cuba including the Bay of Pigs and embargo (B.2.c.iv), and Venezuela's economic ties and oil (C.1.b).
Argentina's conservative restoration (F.1.a), Peron era (F.1.b), and post-1955 military dictatorships, Falklands War, and return to civilian rule (F.1.c) are comprehensively covered.
Brazil's Vargas dictatorship (G.1) and post-1945 developments including military rule, civilian governments, and democratic consolidation (G.2) are comprehensively covered.

Cold War dynamics cover US sponsorship of the Contra insurgency in Nicaragua (B.1.d), Castro's Cuban Revolution and alignment with the Soviet bloc (B.2.c.ii–iii), and the Bay of Pigs invasion and Cuban Missile Crisis (B.2.c.iv).

The Great Depression's economic impact is covered across Ecuador's economic development (D.1.a), Bolivia's mining industry and Chaco War (D.3.a), Chile's economic crises (E.1.b), Argentina's conservative restoration (F.1.a), Uruguay (F.2.a), and Paraguay (F.3.a).
Colombia's Liberal Party rule and social reforms (C.2.a), La Violencia and the National Front (C.2.b), and increasing guerrilla violence and drug trafficking (C.2.c) are comprehensively covered.

Central America's individual state histories including Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Panama, and Belize comprehensively cover the Central American republics since 1920 (B.1).
Mexican wartime cooperation with the US (A.3), Argentina's neutrality (F.1.a), and Brazil's Vargas-era Allied participation (G.1) are directly covered.
The emergence of new Caribbean nations from European colonial rule connects to the new nations of the Caribbean region (B.2.d).
North American history covering Mexico and Central America connects to Mexico since 1920 (A) and Central America and the Caribbean (B).

US imperial interventions including CIA-sponsored coups in Guatemala (B.1.a.ii), Contra insurgency in Nicaragua (B.1.d), and military occupation of Cuba (B.2.c.iv) represent American imperialism in the region.
British insular possessions in the Caribbean (B.2.d) and Britain's defeat of Argentina in the Falklands War (F.1.c) are directly relevant.
Remaining British insular possessions in the Caribbean region connect to the broader British Empire's decolonization (B.2.d).
